title: Sharpening chisels without overthinking it
content: You do not need a 12-stone progression. Here is the two-stone routine I actually use before every joinery session.
excerpt: A no-fuss sharpening routine that works.

title: Hand cutting through dovetails, step by step
content: Layout, sawing to the line, and paring the pins. The whole process on a small drawer with plenty of photos.
excerpt: A full walkthrough of through dovetails.

title: Building a Roubo workbench on a budget
content: You can glue up a solid slab top from construction lumber and still get a bench that will outlast you. Full material list inside.
excerpt: A heavy bench without the hardwood bill.

title: The drawbore joint, and why I trust it
content: Offset the peg hole a hair and the joint pulls itself tight, no clamps needed. Here is how much offset to use.
excerpt: Drawboring for tight, clamp-free joints.

title: A hand-rubbed oil finish that anyone can get right
content: Thin coats, wipe off the excess, let it cure. That is basically it, but the details matter. Full schedule inside.
excerpt: Foolproof oil finishing.

title: Small shop tour: everything on wheels
content: My whole shop lives in a one-car garage. Mobile bases on everything so I can roll it out of the way when I need the floor.
excerpt: How I fit a real shop in a garage.

title: Choosing a jointer plane for flattening
content: A No. 7 gets you long enough to flatten a benchtop and joint an edge. Here is what to look for whether you buy new or vintage.
excerpt: Picking a No. 7 jointer plane.

title: Full build: a walnut hall bench
content: Start to finish on a walnut hall bench with drawbored mortise and tenon joinery and an oil finish. Cut list and photos inside.
excerpt: A complete walnut bench build.

title: Draft: setting up a dust collection run
content: Early notes on ducting a small shop off a single collector. Not finished yet.
excerpt: Work in progress on dust collection.

title: Reading a board: grain direction before you plane
content: Two minutes reading the grain saves an hour fixing tearout. Here is how I check a board before the plane ever touches it.
excerpt: Check grain direction before you plane.

title: Half-blind dovetails for drawer fronts
content: The joint you actually want on a drawer front. Layout is a little fussier than through dovetails but the result hides the end grain.
excerpt: Cutting half-blind dovetails by hand.

title: Flattening a benchtop with a jointer plane
content: Winding sticks, diagonal strokes, then straight passes. Flattening a slab by hand is slower than a machine but a lot quieter.
excerpt: Hand-flattening a benchtop with a No. 7.

title: Wax over oil: the finish I keep coming back to
content: Oil for depth, a coat of paste wax for feel. Simple, repairable, and it looks right on walnut and cherry both.
excerpt: A simple oil-and-wax finish.

title: A leg vise you can build in an afternoon
content: A wooden leg vise with a wooden screw is cheaper than a metal vise and holds work just as well. Here is the whole build.
excerpt: Building a simple wooden leg vise.

title: Chopping mortises by hand without a drill
content: A mortise chisel and a mallet is all it takes. Layout, chopping to depth, and cleaning the walls. Slower than a machine, worth learning.
excerpt: Hand-chopped mortises, step by step.

title: Picking your first three chisels
content: You do not need a full set to start. A quarter, a half, and a three-quarter inch will get you through most joinery. Here is what to buy.
excerpt: The three chisels to start with.

title: Milling rough lumber with hand tools
content: Face, edge, then thickness. Getting a rough board flat and square by hand is the skill everything else stands on. Full sequence inside.
excerpt: Squaring rough stock by hand.

title: Full build: a cherry side table
content: Start to finish on a small cherry side table with tapered legs and a single drawer. Cut list, joinery, and finish schedule inside.
excerpt: A complete cherry side table build.

title: Splined miters for stronger frames
content: A plain miter is weak end grain to end grain. A spline across the corner turns it into a joint you can actually trust. Here is the jig.
excerpt: Reinforcing miters with splines.

title: Sharpening a card scraper properly
content: A well-turned burr on a card scraper leaves a surface no sandpaper matches. Filing, honing, and turning the hook, all of it here.
excerpt: Turning a burr on a card scraper.
